skill.name: nmap
skill.description: Guides authorized nmap reconnaissance, port scanning, service detection, NSE script selection, and scan output organization.
skill.summary: Plan and run authorized nmap scans with structured output and service analysis.
skill.author: BrownFineSecurity

security_audit.summary: Static findings for external commands, sudo usage, and network scanning are true positives in context: the skill instructs agents to run nmap scans, including all-port, stealth, UDP, and vulnerability scans. No prompt injection, credential theft, or covert exfiltration was found, so this is high-risk dual-use security tooling rather than confirmed malicious content.

high_findings.title: Default Active Network Scanning
high_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill tells the agent to run nmap by default, including all 65535 TCP ports and service detection against a user-supplied target. This can probe systems without authorization if the caller supplies an out-of-scope target.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and workflow explicitly instruct execution of nmap scans, not just passive explanation. The authorization reminder appears later, but the default behavior still performs active reconnaissance.

high_findings.title: Stealth and Detection Evasion Guidance
high_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill includes stealth SYN scanning, IDS evasion timing, fragmented packets, and source-port suggestions. These techniques can be used to avoid monitoring on networks the user does not own.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly describes stealth scans and evasion-oriented timing. Legitimate red-team use is possible, but the semantic intent is clearly detection avoidance.

high_findings.title: Vulnerability and Brute Force NSE Script Guidance
high_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill recommends NSE vulnerability checks, brute force script categories, HTTP wildcard scripts, and IoT protocol enumeration. These scans can intensify reconnaissance and may trigger security controls.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The listed nmap scripts are concrete scan categories, including brute and vuln. They are appropriate only inside strict authorization boundaries.

high_findings.title: Privileged Raw Packet Scanning
high_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: Multiple examples require sudo for SYN, UDP, and OS detection scans. Privileged scanning increases host risk and can generate disruptive traffic from the user environment.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sudo commands are explicit and repeated across workflows. The risk is operational and authorization-sensitive rather than evidence of local privilege exploitation.

medium_findings.title: Shell Interpolation Around User Targets
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: Example workflows place target values into shell commands and sometimes use unquoted variables. If an agent copies this pattern with untrusted target text, local shell injection is possible.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples show shell variables and command substitution, but no executable script is packaged. The risk depends on how an agent validates and quotes user-supplied targets.

medium_findings.title: Local Scan Output File Creation
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE WITH LIMITED IMPACT: The skill creates output directories and writes nmap results in multiple formats. This is expected for the tool, but it may store sensitive network inventory on disk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem writes are explicit and purposeful. They are not destructive, but scan results can contain sensitive host and service data.

low_findings.title: Private IP Address Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The hardcoded IP findings are documentation examples using private RFC1918-style addresses for local network ranges. No external callback or fixed third-party target was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The addresses are standard private LAN examples and appear only in nmap usage snippets. They do not indicate exfiltration or a malicious endpoint.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Scanner Hits Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts do not correspond to cryptographic code or commands in the reviewed lines. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, or other weak crypto usage by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited areas showed prose, service tables, and success criteria. The static rule appears to have matched unrelated text rather than actual cryptographic operations.

dangerous_patterns.title: sudo nmap Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Commands repeatedly invoke nmap with sudo for SYN, UDP, OS detection, and retry flows. This pattern enables privileged packet generation from the local machine.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous pattern is directly present in multiple command examples. It is common for nmap, but it materially raises operational risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Stealth and Evasion Flags
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends stealth scan flags and timing profiles that reduce detection. These options are sensitive in a public skill marketplace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly labels stealth and evasion behavior. There is little ambiguity about the purpose of these scan modes.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad NSE Script Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill suggests vulnerability, brute force, auth, discovery, and wildcard HTTP NSE script execution. Broad scripts can produce intrusive probes against targets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands name sensitive NSE categories directly. Impact depends on authorization and target environment.

content.value_statement: Network assessments need repeatable scans, organized output, and clear service summaries. This skill guides authorized nmap workflows for discovery, service detection, and follow-up analysis.
actual_capabilities: Builds a two-phase nmap workflow for port discovery and service detection.
Recommends scan types for TCP, UDP, OS detection, and NSE scripts.
Shows how to save nmap output in normal, XML, and grepable formats.
Parses open ports from saved nmap output for targeted follow-up scans.
Highlights IoT, web, SSH, RTSP, MQTT, and Modbus service checks.
limitations: Requires nmap to be installed on the user machine.
Some scan modes require sudo or equivalent privileges.
Must only be used against systems where scanning is authorized.
Does not exploit vulnerabilities or confirm business impact.

use_cases.title: Authorized Internal Assessment
use_cases.description: Map open ports and service versions on approved internal hosts before a security review.

use_cases.title: IoT Device Enumeration
use_cases.description: Identify camera, MQTT, RTSP, UPnP, and Modbus services on devices within a permitted lab scope.

use_cases.title: Scan Evidence Collection
use_cases.description: Save repeatable nmap outputs and summarize key services for assessment notes and client reporting.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic Scan
prompt_templates.prompt: I have authorization to scan one host. Help me choose a basic nmap command and explain what the results mean.

prompt_templates.title: Run Two-Phase Discovery
prompt_templates.prompt: I have approval for this target. Guide me through a two-phase nmap scan with saved output and service detection.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Saved Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my saved nmap output summary and help identify important services, versions, and follow-up checks.

prompt_templates.title: Design an IoT Test Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Within my authorized IoT lab scope, create an nmap workflow for web, RTSP, MQTT, UPnP, and Modbus services.

output_examples.input: Scan one authorized host and save the results.
output_examples.output: A two-step scan plan with an output directory, all-port discovery, targeted service detection, and a short result summary.

output_examples.input: Open ports show HTTP, RTSP, and MQTT on an IoT camera.
output_examples.output: A prioritized follow-up list for web headers, RTSP methods, MQTT checks, and notes for documenting authorization and scope.

output_examples.input: The target appears down during host discovery.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting path that explains host discovery limits and when an authorized -Pn retry may be appropriate.

best_practices: Confirm written authorization and scope before every scan.
Save outputs in a dedicated directory with clear scan names.
Use targeted follow-up scans after initial port discovery.
anti_patterns: Scanning public or third-party systems without permission.
Running stealth, brute force, or vulnerability scripts outside approved scope.
Leaving scan outputs with sensitive network details in shared locations.

faq.question: Does this skill run nmap automatically?
faq.answer: It provides commands and workflows for an agent or user to run. Execution depends on the connected tool environment.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for production networks?
faq.answer: It can affect production systems. Use conservative timing and only scan approved targets during authorized windows.

faq.question: Why does it mention sudo?
faq.answer: Some nmap scan modes need raw packet access. Sudo increases risk and should be used only when required.

faq.question: Can it test IoT devices?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for common IoT services such as RTSP, MQTT, UPnP, ONVIF-related ports, and Modbus.

faq.question: Does it exploit vulnerabilities?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on reconnaissance and nmap script checks, not exploitation or post-exploitation activity.

faq.question: What should I provide before using it?
faq.answer: Provide the authorized target, scope boundaries, preferred scan depth, timing limits, and output directory requirements.
